AI Palestra.AI Platform

Demo brief
How it works1 · Primary2 · Secondary3 · University4 · IndustryAbout
1

Level 1

Primary School

Real content — IC Bianca pilot, AY 2025/26

A single half-day session, run in parallel across four stations, delivered by Liceo Majorana co-educators to classes of pupils aged 8–11. Below is the real specification and the real POST-evaluation results from the 19 May 2026 event at IC Bianca, Avola.

Half-day, parallel rotation 25–35 min / station 2 co-educators per station Teacher observation + Diario di Bordo Immersive room re-aggregation

Specification

The Four Stations

Stations 1–3 build the human foundations — talent, contribution, success. Station 4 introduces generative AI as an object to be inspected through those foundations: the pupil checks the AI, not the other way around.

1

Talento

Talent & Self-Discovery

Educational objective

Establish that talent is plural, that each pupil has their own, and that discovering personal talent precedes any technical literacy — countering the default school framing of talent as monolithic and competitive.

Materials

Cartelloni illustrati, post-it of multiple colours, pencils, the "Talento" station board. Pupils write/draw one talent per post-it — later re-aggregated in the immersive room.

Facilitation outline

  1. Co-educator opens with a personal story of their own talent discovery.
  2. Pupils write 2–3 talents on post-its.
  3. Round-robin sharing — the co-educator anchors that talents are different and complementary, not ranked.
  4. Closing reflection: "What is something only you can do?"

Expected behavioural outcomes

Pupils articulate at least one talent in their own words, recognise classmates have different talents, and reduce the implicit ranking frame typical of school assessment.

71%

positive station rating

Concept recall — POST, 19 May 2026

52% of pupils named "Ognuno ha talenti diversi" (everyone has different talents) as the lesson they remembered.

2

Valore

From Talent to Contribution

Educational objective

Move from individual identification of talent (Station 1) to the social and ethical question of contribution: my talent has value when it helps someone else.

Materials

Cartelloni illustrati, post-it, the "Valore" station board, the discussion game "Il gioco della Stazione Valore".

Facilitation outline

  1. Co-educator presents three scenarios in which a talent is used — well, badly, neutrally.
  2. Pupils discuss and place post-its with examples of their own talent used to help others.
  3. Closing reflection: "Whose day could you make better with your talent?"

Expected behavioural outcomes

Pupils connect their identified talent to a contribution; vocabulary expands from ability-focused to value-focused — bridging into the ethical dimension of Station 4.

72%

positive station rating

Concept recall — POST, 19 May 2026

51% of pupils named "Il mio talento può aiutare gli altri" (my talent can help others) as the lesson they remembered.

3

Successo

Redefining Success

Educational objective

Surface and gently challenge the default school definition of success (top grade, winning, ranking) and introduce a more research-grounded definition that includes process, growth and meaning.

Materials

Cartelloni illustrati, post-it, the "Successo" station board, the discussion game "Il gioco della Stazione Successo".

Facilitation outline

  1. Co-educator presents two stories — one of effort without external recognition, one with recognition.
  2. Pupils vote post-its on which is the "real" success and discuss.
  3. Co-educator anchors that success has multiple dimensions, of which winning is only one.

Expected behavioural outcomes

Pupils articulate at least two non-victory dimensions of success (effort, learning, helping others) and resist the binary win/lose framing in later conversations.

78%

positive rating — the most liked of the three concept stations

Concept recall — POST, 19 May 2026

61% of pupils named "Il successo non è solo vincere" (success is not only about winning) — the single largest concept-recall figure of the entire POST evaluation.

4

Comic-Strip Review

Human-in-the-Loop

Educational objective

Introduce — concretely, through inspection of real generative outputs — that AI models hallucinate, miss details, get details wrong, and propose stereotyped visuals. The pupil is the corrector, not the consumer.

Materials

Generated comic strips from the 4E pipeline, printed and shown side by side with the source story. Markers and post-its to flag errors against categories: missing detail, wrong detail, stereotype, inconsistency.

Facilitation outline

  1. Co-educator shows a pair: story + AI-generated comic.
  2. Pupils inspect the comic and flag what is wrong, missing or inconsistent.
  3. Discussion of why these errors occur and what it means for trusting AI outputs without checking.
  4. Closing reflection: "When would you check what an AI tells you?"

Expected behavioural outcomes

Pupils develop a habit of inspection rather than acceptance of AI outputs, articulate at least one failure mode of generative models, and transfer that posture beyond comics.

64%

rated "tantissimo" (top mark) — the most liked station of the entire programme

Concept recall — POST, 19 May 2026

1 pupil in 3 independently formulated "l'IA può sbagliare i disegni" (AI can get the drawings wrong) — "Human-in-the-Loop a 10 anni", one of the most generalisable findings of the pilot.

Delivery design

How a session runs

Rotation

The four stations run in parallel; each class rotates through all four during a single half-day session.

Co-educator pairing

Each station is led by two Liceo Majorana students in tandem, to ensure facilitation continuity.

Teacher role

The primary teacher observes and takes the Diario di Bordo Docenti notes — a primary qualitative data source.

Material persistence

Post-its produced at each station travel back to the immersive room, where they're re-aggregated into the closing visual of the session.

Design intent

The pupil must learn to connect AI to the development of their own talent, in order to create value, in order to have a right measure of their personal success.

Without that anchoring, children risk treating AI as a substitute for cognition rather than a complement to it — and the metaphor of palestra (the gym) becomes literal: the three concept stations are the human-formation exercises that keep the mind trained.

See Level 2 — Secondary School →
AI Palestra.AI Platform

Educational architecture for human–AI co-formation. Jointly developed with the schools and partners of the Palestra AI pilot.
